His new company, Ineffable Intelligence, has raised $1.1 billion in seed funding at a $5.1 billion valuation—a significant sum by European standards—and has recruited top researchers from Google DeepMind and other frontier labs. Silver's departure signals a structural disagreement about AI development strategy at a moment when most major labs pursue large language models as the primary route to advanced capabilities.

## The Reinforcement Learning Thesis

Silver argues that LLM-based approaches represent a strategic dead end. Rather than building intelligence from human-generated data—what he characterizes as a "fossil fuel" providing temporary shortcuts—he advocates for AI systems that learn directly through interaction and trial-and-error. His vision centers on placing AI agents inside simulations where they can learn to achieve goals, discover patterns, and collaborate with other agents without relying on human knowledge.

Silver illustrates his concern with a thought experiment: a language model trained on flat-earth data would remain convinced of that belief indefinitely, unable to update through real-world observation. A self-learning system, by contrast, could conduct its own scientific experiments and revise its understanding based on empirical results. He positions this as comparable to pre-Darwinian biology—many observed facts, but no unified principle. Reinforcement learning, in his view, provides that principle for machine intelligence.

## What This Signals for the Sector

Silver's move carries weight because he has demonstrated proof-of-concept at scale. AlphaGo's 2016 victory over world champion Lee Sedol established that reinforcement learning could produce superhuman performance in complex domains. His departure from Google DeepMind—where such work could have continued—underscores his conviction that this approach requires dedicated focus.

The practical implications for enterprises are significant. If Silver's thesis is correct, organizations betting heavily on LLM-based AI for long-term intelligence gains may face capability plateaus. His safety argument also merits attention: AI trained through self-directed learning in controlled environments might present fewer alignment risks than systems inheriting biases from human training data. For practitioners, this suggests monitoring whether simulation-based learning approaches prove more sample-efficient or generate more robust intelligence at scale. The outcome of Ineffable Intelligence will likely influence where the next wave of AI research capital flows.
items.original_title: The Man Behind AlphaGo Thinks AI Is Taking the Wrong Path

items.headline: OpenAI Gains FedRAMP Moderate Clearance for U.S. Government Use
items.body:
OpenAI has achieved FedRAMP Moderate authorization, a security certification that clears ChatGPT Enterprise and the OpenAI API for use across U.S. federal agencies. The authorization removes a significant compliance barrier that has restricted government adoption of generative AI tools, allowing federal IT teams to deploy OpenAI's products without pursuing separate security assessments.

## Federal Compliance Milestone

FedRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) Moderate represents the middle tier of the government's cloud security framework, requiring systems to meet rigorous controls around data handling, encryption, access management, and audit logging. This certification applies to both ChatGPT Enterprise, the commercial chat interface, and the OpenAI API, the programmatic interface used by developers building custom applications.

The authorization signals that OpenAI's infrastructure, operational practices, and security posture have passed the thorough vetting required by federal agencies. Agencies can now procure these services without conducting their own Authority to Operate (ATO) reviews, a process that typically adds months to deployment timelines.

## Practical Impact on Government AI Adoption

The removal of compliance friction addresses one of the main obstacles slowing AI adoption in the public sector. Federal agencies have faced pressure to modernize operations and improve service delivery, but security requirements have made commercial AI tools feel out of reach. With FedRAMP Moderate clearance, procurement becomes simpler and budget cycles move faster.

This authorization does not cover the highest tier, FedRAMP High, which applies to systems handling particularly sensitive data like classified information or biometric records. Agencies with those requirements will still need to pursue specialized authorizations. However, the majority of federal workflows—from policy analysis to constituent communications to internal process automation—operate within the Moderate security boundary.

For enterprise AI practitioners watching government deployment patterns, FedRAMP clearance typically signals a maturation point in the vendor lifecycle. Once certified, vendors usually see accelerated adoption as government procurement teams reduce perceived risk and legal departments gain confidence. The move also indicates OpenAI is committed to long-term government market positioning, not treating federal sales as secondary to commercial work. Organizations seeking OpenAI integration at agencies should expect faster approval cycles and reduced negotiation friction around security requirements.
items.original_title: OpenAI available at FedRAMP Moderate
